confirmed.title: Three near-identical base64 multi-variant decoders
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. tryDecodeBase64 (decoder.go:15-37) and decodeBase64WithPadding (node_parser.go:379-397) are the same 4-variant sequence (URL-noPad, Std-noPad, URL-pad, Std-pad), differing only in return signature (tryDecodeBase64 also returns a source-description string). tryBase64Decode (meta.go:381-404) is a third variant: same 4 encodings (expressed as Std/RawStd/URL/RawURL) plus UTF-8 + control-char validation, returning a trimmed string. Adding a new encoding would indeed require edits in 3 places. Severity medium is slightly high for pure-helper near-dupes with no behavioral drift risk (decode is idempotent), but the consolidation is legitimate.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Extract decodeBase64Any([]byte) ([]byte, ok); have decodeBase64WithPadding + tryDecodeBase64 delegate; keep tryBase64Decode as a UTF-8/control-char-validating wrapper.
confirmed.verifyReason: Read all three: decoder.go:15-37 and node_parser.go:379-397 are byte-identical attempt order; meta.go:381-404 is the same idea with validation. Downgraded medium→low (no drift risk, pure helpers).

confirmed.title: Deprecated FetchSubscription duplicates the entire fetch pipeline of FetchSubscriptionWithMeta

It IS still the live cache-miss network path: source_loader.go:123 calls FetchSubscription. Note the suggested 'thin wrapper returning res.Body' would change observable behavior on the legacy path (it would start returning announce/HTTP-typed errors) — a net improvement but a behavior change, so not purely mechanical.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Make FetchSubscription call FetchSubscriptionWithMeta and return res.Body; or migrate source_loader.go:123 to WithMeta and delete FetchSubscription. Verify callers don't depend on the old flat error strings.
confirmed.verifyReason: fetcher.go:343-419 duplicates the 226-299 pipeline; source_loader.go:123 still calls it on cache-miss; the two error paths genuinely diverge (announce handling only in WithMeta).

confirmed.title: errEmptyBody sentinel is never returned — errors.Is check is dead
confirmed.detail: Verified: `errEmptyBody` appears only at its definition (lines 385-387) and in the `errors.Is(err, errEmptyBody{})` clause at line 375 — grep across the whole repo finds no site that returns or wraps it. The json.Decoder returns io.EOF / io.ErrUnexpectedEOF, never errEmptyBody, so errors.Is(err, errEmptyBody{}) is structurally always false; only the strings.Contains("EOF") branch can fire. The doc-comment at 383-384 explicitly calls it a 'placeholder ... kept as a typed sentinel so a future refactor can switch on it.' The type, its Error() method, and the errors.Is clause are all dead.

confirmed.verifyReason: grep shows errEmptyBody used only at def + the errors.Is clause; runtime test confirms empty body yields io.EOF, never errEmptyBody, so the clause is always false (dead).

confirmed.title: Truncated request bodies silently swallowed via strings.Contains(err,"EOF")
confirmed.detail: Confirmed by runtime test: `json.NewDecoder(strings.NewReader(`{"final":"x`)).Decode(...)` returns io.ErrUnexpectedEOF whose Error() is 'unexpected EOF' — which contains the substring 'EOF'. decodeJSONBody therefore returns nil (treats it as empty body) instead of propagating a 400. For an endpoint where the zero value is a destructive request (PATCH /state/dns replaces the WHOLE dns_options section; an empty/zero DNSOptions = clear servers+rules per API.md:88), a truncated mid-transfer body is silently accepted as a destructive clear. This is exactly the 'accidental wipe from truncated requests' the settings_endpoints.go:48-50 comment claims to guard against. Note: /state/dns uses its own decode path, but decodeJSONBody is the shared helper for /traffic/* POST bodies and is the pattern that should be fixed.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Match only the true empty-body case with `errors.Is(err, io.EOF)` and let io.ErrUnexpectedEOF (and every other decode error) propagate as a 400. Drop the substring match.
confirmed.verifyReason: Ran the exact case: truncated JSON returns 'unexpected EOF', strings.Contains(...,"EOF")=true, so decodeJSONBody swallows it as empty — real silent-acceptance of malformed bodies.

confirmed.title: AutoLoadProxies leaks AutoLoadInProgress=true when group is cleared mid-retry
confirmed.detail: Confirmed real. The `if currentGroup == ""` branch at lines 315-318 does a bare `return` inside the retry goroutine without resetting AutoLoadInProgress=false, while every other exit path (280-285, 290-296, 371-374, 377-380) clears it under AutoLoadMutex. If the flag leaks, all future AutoLoadProxies calls hit the 'Already in progress' early-return at 249-253 forever. Severity tempered to medium: currentGroup is only set to "" by ReloadClashAPIConfig (line 220) on a config-load failure, which is an uncommon edge path, not the normal flow. But once it does fire, the launcher silently stops auto-loading proxies until restart, which is a real user-visible degradation. The suggested defer-based fix is the clean remedy.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Wrap the goroutine body in `defer func(){ apiSvc.AutoLoadMutex.Lock(); apiSvc.AutoLoadInProgress=false; apiSvc.AutoLoadMutex.Unlock() }()` so no exit path can leak the flag; then drop the four scattered manual resets.
confirmed.verifyReason: api_service.go:315-318 bare `return` confirmed; all other exits clear flag; early-guard at 249-253 makes the leak permanent. Severity lowered from high (rare trigger via ReloadClashAPIConfig:220).

confirmed.title: Unsynchronized read of apiSvc.Enabled races with ReloadClashAPIConfig
confirmed.detail: Confirmed as an unsynchronized field access. Line 257 `if !apiSvc.Enabled` reads Enabled with no lock; ReloadClashAPIConfig writes Enabled/BaseURL/Token/SelectedClashGroup under StateMutex.Lock() (lines 209-242) and GetClashAPIConfig reads under RLock (201-203). ReloadClashAPIConfig is invoked from process_service.go:172 (sing-box start path) while AutoLoadProxies runs from several goroutines (process_service.go:231,298, tray_menu.go:146 `go ac.AutoLoadProxies()`, clash_api_tab.go:1290). A concurrent Reload+AutoLoad is plausible, so this is a genuine data race on a plain bool. Medium is fair — worst case is a stale read of one bool that only gates a skip-log, not memory corruption, but `-race` would flag it.

confirmed.title: buildConfigForPreview copies displayBody.Options wholesale, leaking urltest keys into selector configs
confirmed.detail: VERIFIED, and impact is understated. At 387-391, when displayBody.Options is non-nil ALL existing Options keys are copied into cfg regardless of the chosen Type. The urltest-overwrite block (409-434) only runs `if obType == "urltest"`, and the clean type-default else-branches (392-400) are bypassed whenever Options already exists. So flipping an existing urltest → selector (manual) carries stale url/interval/tolerance into a selector cfg.Options. The generator emits ALL Options keys verbatim for any type (outbound_generator.go:640-646 loops every key), so the produced config.json would contain url/interval/tolerance under "type":"selector" — which sing-box's selector outbound does not accept, risking a parse/start failure. Real config-correctness bug, not cosmetic; severity arguably medium given it can yield a config sing-box rejects (requires the specific edit-existing-urltest→flip-to-manual→save flow).
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: When copying existing Options for a selector, drop type-incompatible keys (url/interval/tolerance), or rebuild Options from the type-appropriate default and carry over only keys valid for the chosen type.
confirmed.verifyReason: Confirmed wholesale copy at :387-391 bypasses type-default branches; generator at outbound_generator.go:640-646 emits every Options key verbatim, so a flipped selector would carry urltest-only keys into config.json — raised severity low→medium.

confirmed.title: rebuildFromChips silently overwrites a hand-typed regex when no chips are selected
confirmed.detail: VERIFIED. rebuildFromChips (235-244) builds `picked` from the selected map and calls regexEntry.SetText(buildFlagRegex(picked, excludeCheck.Checked)). buildFlagRegex returns "" when len(selected)==0 (104-106). excludeCheck.OnChanged (245) calls rebuildFromChips. So if the user types a manual regex and then toggles Exclude with no chips selected, SetText("") wipes the typed filter. The chip→regex direction is destructive over manual edits in that path. Minor UX papercut.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Skip SetText when picked is empty (preserve manual regex), or make the Exclude toggle a no-op while no chips are selected.
confirmed.verifyReason: buildFlagRegex returns "" for empty selection (:104-106) and excludeCheck.OnChanged→rebuildFromChips→SetText("") at :242,:245 will overwrite a hand-typed regex; raised confidence medium→high.

confirmed.title: filteredIndices() recomputed O(n) per rendered row → O(rows×n) per Live refresh
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. live_view.go:84 (length cb) and :91 (per-row update cb) both call filteredIndices(), an O(n) scan over up to liveViewRingSize=5000 events (const at :63). list.Refresh() fires on every incoming event (:243), and OnSelected (:124) calls it again. So each refresh re-scans the ring once for length + once per visible row. The claim is accurate; the only nuance is the cost is bounded by liveViewRingSize (5000) and the handful of rows Fyne actually renders (the visible viewport, ~20-30), not all 5000 rows — so it is O(visible×5000) per event, not O(5000²). Still wasteful and the suggested per-refresh cache (invalidated on filter/events change) is a clean fix.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Compute filteredIndices() once per refresh, cache it on v under v.mu, invalidate when filter or events change, and have length + update + OnSelected read the cached slice.
confirmed.verifyReason: live_view.go:84 and :91 both invoke filteredIndices() (O(n) over 5000-cap ring); Refresh fires per-event at :243 — real redundant rescans, bounded by visible rows not n².

confirmed.title: Per-process view re-aggregates the entire session on every single event with no coalescing
confirmed.detail: Confirmed and arguably understated on copy count. The subscribe pump (:300-304) calls fyne.Do(v.refresh) for EVERY event with no debounce. refresh() (:239-248) does active.Events() (full copy of up to maxEventsPerSession=50000, session.go:10/:69) AND AggregateDomains/IPs/Conns — and each Aggregate* itself calls s.Events() internally (session.go:151,194,229), so refresh copies the entire event slice 4 times per call, plus 3 sort.Slice, then Refreshes 5 lists. The 1s ticker (:285-296) already drives liveness, so the per-event refresh is pure duplication. The subscribe channel is buffered 256 (profiler.go:511) and drops on backpressure, which caps the worst case, but a busy session still re-runs the whole 4×-copy+aggregate+sort pipeline far more than needed.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Drop the per-event fyne.Do(v.refresh); set an atomic dirty flag in the subscriber and let only the 1s ticker coalesce (skip aggregation when not dirty).
confirmed.verifyReason: per_process_view.go:302 refreshes per event; refresh() at :239-248 copies events 4× (Events() + 3 Aggregates each re-call Events()) + 3 sorts + 5 list.Refresh, duplicating the 1s ticker.

confirmed.title: Per-process 1s ticker re-aggregates even when idle / showing a read-only saved session
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. The ticker (:285-296) calls fyne.Do(v.refresh) every second for the window's whole lifetime, unconditionally. When idle (target=="", :262-269) refresh re-runs reverseSessions(deps.Profiler.CompletedSessions()) each tick; when showing a read-only saved session (target set, active==nil, :270-272) it re-renders a static view every second. refresh() always ends with 5 list.Refresh() calls (:274-278) regardless. Aggregation/render only matters while a session actively records or its timer ticks. Low severity is correct — the work is small when idle (no active.Events()) but it is genuinely needless spin.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Gate the ticker body on deps.Profiler.ActiveSession() != nil, or start/stop the ticker on session start/stop, so idle and read-only states don't re-render every second.
confirmed.verifyReason: Ticker at :285-296 fires refresh() unconditionally; idle branch :267 re-runs CompletedSessions+reverseSessions and :274-278 Refreshes 5 lists every second even with no active session.

confirmed.title: Session aggregation rescans full event slice (up to 50k) on every UI refresh tick
confirmed.detail: Verified the cost and the cadence. ui/traffic/per_process_view.go:286 starts a time.NewTicker(time.Second) whose callback (refresh, lines 223-281 / the active-session block 239-248) calls Events() once plus AggregateDomains/IPs/Conns each tick. Each Aggregate* calls Events() again (session.go:151,194,229) — a full RLock copy of up to 50k events — then rebuilds maps from scratch, using O(n) appendUnique inside per-event loops (session.go:176-180,215-217,268-270) giving O(n*k) per group. No caching exists. So the per-second tick does ~4 full 50k-element copies + 3 full rebuilds. Corrections to the finding: cadence is 1 Hz (not per-event), and the per-event inner loops are O(n*k) over distinct IPs/outbounds per group rather than strictly O(n^2) over all events — still the heaviest per-tick work in the module, and worst at subscription scale.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Cache aggregation results keyed by event count / last-append (recompute only when the slice grew), or maintain incremental indices. Also fix the stale comment (see separate finding).
confirmed.verifyReason: Confirmed: 1 Hz ticker drives 4x full-slice copy + 3 full map rebuilds with no cache; real per-tick cost that scales with the 50k cap, though cadence is per-second not per-event.

confirmed.title: dispatch dnsByIP sweep uses e.TS as 'now', skewing eviction for late/backfilled events
confirmed.detail: Verified the inconsistency. The sweep at profiler.go:350-357 computes cutoffDNS := e.TS.Add(-dnsInferWindow) using the triggering event's timestamp, while the inference read at eventFromConn:224 uses `at` (poll wall-clock) for the same map's window. A log event with an older parsed TS shifts the eviction cutoff backwards and under-evicts. It only runs past the 2048-entry threshold (dnsByIPSweepThreshold) so the practical impact is small, but two clocks govern one TTL map.
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Use time.Now() (or one consistent clock) for the dnsByIP TTL sweep so it matches the inference-window check.
confirmed.verifyReason: Confirmed: sweep uses e.TS while inference uses `at`; inconsistent clocks on one map, though gated behind the 2048 threshold so impact is minor.

confirmed.title: Redundant & 0xff mask after uint8 cast in hover blend
confirmed.detail: Confirmed. mix() at hover_row.go:149-150 is `return uint8(((x*wb + y*wp)/100)>>8) & 0xff`. The & 0xff is applied to a value already cast to uint8 (range 0-255), so it is a no-op. confirmed.title: debuglog.Log formats the same message up to three times
confirmed.detail: Confirmed but trivial. In Log() when prefix!="" and fileLog: line 98 fmt.Sprintf(format,args) -> message; line 101 fmt.Sprintf("[%s] %s",prefix,message) -> line; line 103 log.Printf("[%s] %s",prefix,message) re-formats the prefixed string a second time. So the prefixed string is built twice. Additionally `line` is always constructed (99-110) but only consumed by the viewer-sink branch (112), so when only fileLog is true it is built and discarded. Reusing line via log.Print(line) and gating its construction on viewerWants would avoid the duplicate work. Minor; not a hot path that would matter at default Warn level.

confirmed.title: apiLogFile written/read without synchronization (data race) while sibling sink is locked
confirmed.detail: Confirmed genuine race. apiLogFile is a plain package var (line 194). SetAPILogFile writes it with no lock (line 203). writeLog reads it and Fprintf's with no lock (lines 226-227). writeLog runs on background request goroutines (each Clash call logs), while SetAPILogFile(nil) is called during shutdown (main.go:387, core/controller.go:381) and at init (controller.go:207). The adjacent apiLogSink IS guarded by apiLogSinkMu (lines 196-199, 229-231), so the file pointer was overlooked. Two real hazards: unsynchronized concurrent read/write of the var, and use-after-close of the *os.File if writeLog races a shutdown SetAPILogFile(nil)+Close. The audit cited main.go:387 only; there is a second nil-writer at core/controller.go:381 which strengthens the claim. Severity medium is fair (data race, but in a log path during shutdown, low blast radius).
confirmed.suggestedCleanup: Guard apiLogFile with apiLogSinkMu (or a dedicated mutex): lock in SetAPILogFile; snapshot the *os.File under RLock in writeLog before Fprintf.
confirmed.verifyReason: clash.go:203 writes apiLogFile lock-free, clash.go:226-227 reads+Fprintf lock-free on request goroutines, while SetAPILogFile(nil) fires at shutdown (main.go:387, controller.go:381) — sibling apiLogSink uses apiLogSinkMu, confirming the file var was missed.

judgment.title: Crash/restart state machine duplicated between Monitor and onPrivilegedScriptExited
judgment.detail: Verified: onPrivilegedScriptExited (305-369) and Monitor (372-478) implement the same post-exit ladder: StoppedByUser reset; RestartRequestedByUser -> runGhostTunCleanup(true)+Start(true); ConsecutiveCrashAttempts++; >restartAttempts -> error dialog; else 2s wait + Start(true) + stability-threshold goroutine that resets the counter. They have already drifted (Monitor has the PID-changed obsolescence check at 386-389 and the err==nil graceful-exit branch at 419-425, which the privileged path lacks because the script wait gives no exit code). Extracting a shared handleProcessExit would reduce drift, but the edges genuinely differ (privileged kill vs cmd kill, PID tracking), so this is a refactor judgment call for the owner, not a clear-cut defect. Severity medium is reasonable given the safety-critical nature of the restart loop.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Extract a shared handleProcessExit(reason) helper both paths funnel into, keeping only privileged-vs-cmd specifics at the edges.
judgment.verifyReason: Both functions verified present with the duplicated ladder; drift is real (Monitor-only err==nil branch at 419-425 + PID check at 386). Whether to unify is an architecture/taste call given the diverging kill/exit edges.

judgment.title: IsNetworkError relies on deprecated net.Error.Temporary()
judgment.detail: Verified: line 81 calls netErr.Temporary(). net.Error.Temporary() was deprecated in Go 1.18 because its semantics are ill-defined/unreliable. The function already handles Timeout(), *net.OpError, *net.DNSError, and context cancel/deadline, so dropping the Temporary() branch loses little. However this is a behavior-affecting change (some errors currently classified as network errors via Temporary would stop being), so it is a judgment call rather than a pure cleanup.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Drop the Temporary() branch, or replace with explicit errors.Is checks for the cases that matter.
judgment.verifyReason: Confirmed Temporary() call at line 81; deprecation is factual, but removal changes classification behavior, so owner should decide. Bumped confidence high (the deprecation is verifiable fact).

judgment.title: GetController fallback path re-implements NewAppController wiring (two init sites that drift)

It omits UIService, APIService, OnWindowShown callback, and the auto-update loop, so new required wiring in NewAppController must be remembered here. The comment itself admits 'this should never happen in normal operation'. Real maintenance hazard, but it is defensive fallback code on a 'should never happen' path, so whether to delete vs keep is an owner call.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Drop the fallback construction and return nil (callers already nil-check GetController), or factor shared service wiring into one helper both call.
judgment.verifyReason: Fallback block at 145-164 verified to duplicate a subset of NewAppController:192-305 wiring and omit UIService/APIService/auto-update; removal vs retention of defensive path is a design decision.

judgment.title: NewConfigService mutates package-global function vars as a constructor side effect
judgment.detail: Verified: NewConfigService (37-71) reassigns subscription.CreateHTTPClientFunc (38), subscription.IsNetworkErrorFunc (39), subscription.GetNetworkErrorMessageFunc (40), subscription.LoadSubscriptionSettingsFunc (46-68), and services.CreateHTTPClientFunc (69) on every call. It is called from NewAppController (226), the GetController fallback (159), and tests, so these globals are clobbered repeatedly and the LoadSubscriptionSettingsFunc closure captures whichever 'ac' constructed last. In practice the launcher constructs one controller, so the clobbering is benign at runtime, but it is a real constructor-side-effect smell that can bite tests running in parallel.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Move the one-time global wiring into a sync.Once or into NewAppController, leaving NewConfigService side-effect-free.
judgment.verifyReason: Lines 38-69 confirm five package-global func-var assignments inside the constructor; the captured-ac closure at 46-68 is real. Impact is low (single controller in production) so cleanup is a taste/test-hygiene call.

judgment.title: GetController emits a WARN log on its normal first-call path
judgment.detail: Verified the WarnLog at line 142 fires whenever instance is nil. However the premise is overstated: in normal operation NewAppController runs in main.go before any GetController call, so instance is non-nil and the warn never fires. The 'normal first-call path' framing is misleading — the warn only fires if GetController is genuinely called before NewAppController (a real bug condition) or in tests. As an actual diagnostic for a 'should never happen' state, a WARN is arguably appropriate, not noise.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optionally demote to DebugLog, or gate behind a known-initialized flag — but this is marginal.
judgment.verifyReason: Warn at controller.go:142 is real, but it does NOT fire on the normal path (NewAppController sets instance before main uses GetController); the finding's 'normal first-call' claim is inaccurate, so this is at most a minor logging-taste call.

judgment.title: Watchdog goroutine has a no-op _ = pInfo after fetching process info it never uses
judgment.detail: Partially verified but premise overstated. The watchdog (556-567) calls process.FindProcess(pid) and discards pInfo via '_ = pInfo' at 560. However FindProcess (internal/process/process.go:29-40) enumerates ALL processes via ps.Processes() to determine existence — that enumeration is unavoidable for the boolean check, and pInfo is a trivial {PID,Name} struct, NOT 'extra work'. There is no lighter existence API in the package. So the only real nit is the explanatory '_ = pInfo' line being slightly noisy; the 'FindProcess does extra work to populate pInfo' rationale is incorrect.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Replace 'pInfo, found, err := ...' with '_, found, err := ...' and drop the '_ = pInfo' line. No perf gain.
judgment.verifyReason: Code at 558-560 confirmed, but FindProcess (process.go:30) already enumerates all procs regardless of pInfo use; the 'extra work' justification is false, leaving only a cosmetic '_ =' tidy-up.

judgment.title: getReleaseInfoFromSourceForge accepts ctx but never uses it
judgment.detail: Verified: getReleaseInfoFromSourceForge(ctx context.Context, version string) at 186-194 ignores ctx — the body only calls buildSourceForgeAssets(version) (which takes no ctx) and returns a struct. The function is pure/synchronous so there is nothing to cancel; cancellation bites later in the actual download. Keeping ctx for signature symmetry with getReleaseInfoFromGitHub is a defensible choice. Minor.

judgment.verifyReason: Body at 187-193 never references ctx; param is unused. Whether to drop or keep-for-symmetry is a style call (and dropping requires touching the caller's fallback branch).

judgment.title: parseCSVLine trailing-field condition is subtly wrong / over-clever
judgment.detail: Verified the function at 658-683 and the trailing condition 'if current.Len() > 0 || len(parts) > 0' at 679. It is NOT actually wrong — the OR condition is what makes both the single-token and trailing-comma cases work. The 'subtly wrong' framing is inaccurate; it is merely a hand-rolled parser where encoding/csv would do. Low-value taste finding; confidence in the original 'wrong' claim is low.

judgment.verifyReason: Traced the loop: condition at 679 yields correct results for token/trailing-comma/empty inputs, so 'subtly wrong' is refuted; only the readability/over-clever angle stands as a taste call.

judgment.title: MergeDNSSection dns.final fallback can pick a tag that was filtered out of dns.servers[]
judgment.detail: The code shape is real: emitted servers loop skips body-less {tag,enabled} markers via !dnsServerHasBody (line 79-81), but firstEnabledDNSServerTag (174-190) only checks dnsServerEnabled, not dnsServerHasBody, so it CAN return a marker tag absent from dns.servers[]. BUT the reachability is now near-nil: the only producer of body-less markers in cfg.Servers was legacyDNSOptionsFromV6, which is УДАЛЕНА in SPEC 056-R-N (state/load.go:287). On the live v6 path cfg.Servers is always empty (config_service.go:436-439 sets only Final/Strategy); on pure-v5 cfg.Servers = s.DNSOptions.Servers, which are full user server objects with bodies. So the divergence exists in code but is effectively unreachable with current state shapes — a latent robustness gap, not an active bug.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Cheap hardening: make firstEnabledDNSServerTag also require dnsServerHasBody(o), or compute fallback from the already-built `servers` slice. Owner's call whether worth it given no live trigger.
judgment.verifyReason: Code asymmetry confirmed, but the marker-producer was deleted (legacyDNSOptionsFromV6 УДАЛЕНА) and v6 cfg.Servers is empty, so practically unreachable.

judgment.title: DNS section is parsed and re-marshaled twice on every build (MergeDNSSection then MergePresetsIntoDNS)
judgment.detail: Confirmed: buildSection 'dns' calls MergeDNSSection (unmarshal→mutate→json.Marshal, dns_merge.go:51-109) then MergePresetsIntoDNS (unmarshal again→mutate→json.MarshalIndent, preset_merge.go:263-347). On the v6 path cfg.Servers is empty (config_service.go:436-439) so MergeDNSSection's server loop is near-empty yet still pays the full parse+marshal round-trip; same shape for route. Real redundant work, but it runs once per Save/Update/preview (not per-proxy, not in any hot loop), so impact is negligible — a tidiness/efficiency call, not a measurable bottleneck.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optional: when v6 path active, pass the parsed map through to MergePresetsInto* instead of re-serializing/re-parsing. Low priority.
judgment.verifyReason: Double parse/marshal confirmed, but it is once-per-build (cold path), so 'bottleneck' framing is overstated; downgraded to a judgment call.

judgment.detail: Confirmed. osStatLocal/urlParseLocal/sha256SumLocal are one-line wrappers over os.Stat/url.Parse/sha256.Sum256, each used at exactly one call site (osStatLocal@62, urlParseLocal@146, sha256SumLocal@161). judgment.title: VMess buildOutbound re-implements ws/grpc/http transport instead of reusing uriTransportFromQuery
judgment.detail: Confirmed real, needs owner judgment. The vmess switch (646-706) builds httpupgrade/h2/ws/http/grpc transport maps; uriTransportFromQuery (65-136) builds ws/grpc/http/httpupgrade with the same path/service_name/Host-list logic. However the two are NOT drop-in equivalent: (a) vmess reads keys via node.Query.Get + queryGetFold(host->sni) fallbacks that uriTransportFromQuery does not replicate for ws (it falls back host->sni->obfsParam) and grpc (it falls back serviceName->service_name->path); (b) the h2 case is genuinely vmess-only (uriTransportFromQuery returns nil for h2 at line 131); (c) httpupgrade host is a plain string in both but vmess adds an sni fallback the shared one lacks (127-129 vs 652-658). So delegating requires reconciling several small key-fallback differences — confidence medium is right, and this is a refactor-with-care, not a mechanical merge.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Reconcile the fallback chains, then have vmess delegate ws/http/grpc/httpupgrade to uriTransportFromQuery and keep only the h2 special-case; add regression tests for the host/sni/serviceName fallbacks first.
judgment.verifyReason: Both build the same transport types but with different key-fallback chains (ws host->sni vs host->sni->obfsParam; grpc path-only vs serviceName/service_name/path), so it's a careful merge, not a clean dedupe; downgraded medium→low.

judgment.title: Misleading final error message in DecodeSubscriptionContent
judgment.detail: Confirmed but minor. Line 99 returns `fmt.Errorf("failed to decode base64 content: %w", err)` where err is the base64 error captured at line 53. By the time control reaches 99, the content has also failed JSON-array (76-85), JSON-object (88-91), and plain-text '://' (94-97) checks, so the true cause is 'unrecognized subscription format', not specifically a base64 failure. The base64 framing can misdirect triage. Genuinely a taste/wording call — the wrapped err still carries the base64 detail, so it's not strictly wrong, just narrow.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Return 'unrecognized subscription content (not base64, JSON array, or plain-text links)'.
judgment.verifyReason: decoder.go:99 wraps the base64 err for content that also failed JSON/plain-text checks; the message is narrow but not factually false — owner's call on wording.

judgment.title: LoadNodesFromSource is a ~230-line function with deep nesting and pervasive debuglog timing noise
judgment.detail: Confirmed. LoadNodesFromSource spans 83-312 (~230 lines) with branches for cache-hook, Xray-JSON-array, line-by-line URI, legacy direct-link-in-Source, and Connections[]. judgment.title: diff.go and sync_dns.go are not gofmt-clean (CI/lint risk)
judgment.detail: Partially verified, claim overstated. `gofmt -l` does flag both files: the Diff struct fields are misaligned (gofmt -d shows realignment of ProxiesChanged/VarsChanged/etc), diff.go has a trailing blank line at EOF (line 261), and sync_dns.go:14-24 uses leading-space comment indentation that gofmt rewrites to tab-indented. HOWEVER the 'CI/lint risk' framing is wrong: .golangci.yaml enables only govet/ineffassign/unused/errcheck/whitespace/bodyclose plus revive — it does NOT enable gofmt/gofumpt/gci/goimports formatters, so the struct misalignment and comment indentation are NOT gated by this repo's CI. Only the trailing blank line at diff.go EOF might trip the `whitespace` linter. So this is a cosmetic gofmt cleanup, not a CI gate failure.

judgment.title: Confusing edge-case handling in syncConnectionsFromLegacy nil-Proxies branch
judgment.detail: Verified: adapter.go:31-41 — when ParserConfig.Proxies==nil, the function calls syncLegacyFromConnections(s) then returns, with a 6-line comment (lines 35-40) explicitly noting that syncLegacyFromConnections will set Proxies to non-nil and that 'in this edge case caller обычно делает один Save'. judgment.title: Hand-rolled ULID base32 encoder uses 16 magic-number bit-twiddling lines

judgment.title: Two parallel scalar-stringify implementations for default values
judgment.detail: Confirmed both exist and overlap, but the framing overstates the risk. defaultValueStringify (vars_default.go:151-169) takes a decoded interface{} (string/json.Number/float64/bool/nil) and is used for already-unmarshaled values. readJSONLiteralAsString (vars_resolve.go:349-373) probes raw json.RawMessage bytes by trying successive Unmarshal targets, used for default_node path extraction. They solve adjacent-but-distinct problems (decoded value vs raw bytes), so this is a reasonable refactor opportunity, not a logic divergence — outputs agree on the shared cases (both use FormatFloat 'f',-1,64 and 'true'/'false'). Taste call.

judgment.title: Redundant config size re-check after full file read
judgment.detail: Mostly confirmed but borderline. fileInfo.Size() is checked at 25-27 before os.ReadFile (29); the re-check at 34-37 tests len(data) > MaxConfigFileSize. After a successful full read len(data) == fileInfo.Size(), so in the normal path it can never fire. There is a theoretical TOCTOU angle (file grows between stat and read could make len(data) > Size), but os.ReadFile pre-sizes its buffer from the stat and a grown file would still be caught here — so it offers negligible extra safety. The third check at 46-48 on the extracted JSON is meaningful and the finding correctly says to keep it. This is taste/defensiveness, hence verdict needs-judgment rather than a clear bug; severity low is right.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Delete the if len(data) > MaxConfigFileSize block (34-37); the stat check (25-27) and extracted-JSON check (46-48) suffice.
judgment.verifyReason: Read factory.go:21-48 — len(data) equals fileInfo.Size() after a full ReadFile so the check is dead in the normal path, but it is a harmless defensive guard with a marginal TOCTOU justification, so removal is the owner's call.

judgment.title: Unreachable int/int64 branches in JSON-decoded number coercers
judgment.detail: Confirmed as written but low value. xrayJSONInt (31-40), mapGetInt (91-97), mapGetBool (120-128) and wireGuardJSONElemToString (786-794) all carry `case int:`/`case int64:` arms. Values reaching these come from json.Unmarshal into map[string]interface{}/[]interface{}, which yields float64 or json.Number for numbers, never int/int64 — so those arms are unreachable in practice. However mapGetString/mapGetInt are general-purpose helpers that COULD in principle be handed a programmatically-built map with int values (e.g. the throwaway-map idiom in finding #9 passes h[0] which is interface{}), so calling them strictly dead requires assuming all inputs are JSON-decoded. Harmless defensiveness; confidence medium as the prior agent stated. Owner call.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optionally drop the int/int64 arms (keep float64 + json.Number). Very low priority.
judgment.verifyReason: Read all four switches; for JSON-decoded inputs the int/int64 arms are indeed unreachable, but these are exported-ish general helpers so the contract isn't strictly impossible — removal is a taste call, not a clear win.

judgment.title: GenerateNodeJSON / GenerateSelectorWithFilteredAddOutbounds hand-build JSON via string concatenation

GenerateNodeJSON (from 242) assembles outbounds by appending fmt.Sprintf'd `"key":value` fragments into a []string (parts) with per-field marshalJSONString and manual nested-object building. The rationale — field-order control + trailing `// label` comment line — is real, and the sibling GenerateEndpointJSON already uses json.MarshalIndent, showing the marshal approach is viable. Severity low / confidence medium as stated.

judgment.verifyReason: Read GenerateNodeJSON from outbound_generator.go:242 — confirmed []string + fmt.Sprintf fragment assembly; the field-order/comment rationale is real and the rewrite is large with golden-test impact, so it's a taste/owner call.

A map[string]struct{} seen-set would be cleaner and preserve order. Minor taste improvement.

judgment.title: http.MaxBytesReader called with nil ResponseWriter
judgment.detail: Confirmed present: decodeJSONBody calls `http.MaxBytesReader(nil, r.Body, 1<<20)` at line 370. Passing nil is valid on modern Go (the writer is only used to set the close-connection signal on overflow); with nil that proactive-close hint is lost, but the read still errors on over-limit so the size cap itself works. The handler signature has no `w`, so threading the real writer needs a signature change. This is a minor robustness/taste call, not a correctness bug — owner's judgment whether the proactive close is worth the refactor or just a clarifying comment.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Either thread w into decodeJSONBody to pass the real ResponseWriter (clean over-limit connection close), or add a one-line comment that nil is intentional. Low priority.
judgment.verifyReason: Code matches (nil writer at line 370); the size cap works, only the connection-close-on-overflow hint is lost — a robustness nicety, not a defect.

judgment.title: API.md says '24 endpoints' but 25 routes are registered
judgment.detail: Partially confirmed: server.go registers exactly 25 distinct mux paths (verified via grep: /ping, /version, /state, /proxies, /debug/snapshot, 5 /action/*, 5 /state/*, /settings/user-agent, and 9 /traffic/* including both /traffic/sessions and /traffic/sessions/). However the count is convention-dependent: /traffic/sessions and /traffic/sessions/ are the same logical endpoint (the trailing-slash handler dispatches GET/DELETE /traffic/sessions/{id}), and collapsing them gives 24 — which is what the prose summary likely intends. Meanwhile the doc's own method-table lists MORE than 24 rows (it counts GET+PATCH on the same path separately). So '24' is a loose round-number summary, not a precise route count. Real-but-trivial doc nit.

judgment.title: ProxyActiveChanged is subscribed but never published — dead handler path

judgment.title: Over-verbose / mixed-language doc blocks on small accessors
judgment.detail: Partially confirmed but overstated, and the framing is off. The cited state_service.go:11-19 block is the DefaultAutoPingMaxProxies const doc — a substantive field-report rationale (the 500-node Windows storm), NOT a trivial getter; that comment earns its length. state_service.go:68-71 (LastUpdateSucceededAt) is a 2-line Russian note, mild. api_service.go:347-364 is the Russian inline narration around the save-last-selected switch logic — genuinely verbose Russian-only commentary. judgment.title: SetTemplatePreviewText wraps an already-async UpdateUI in an extra goroutine for large text
judgment.detail: Premise verified: UpdateUI → SafeFyneDo → fyne.Do(fn) (presenter.go:103,110) is asynchronous queueing onto the Fyne loop. judgment.title: SaveInProgress set redundantly in SaveConfig (direct + via SetSaveState)
judgment.detail: Verified. SaveConfig sets `p.guiState.SaveInProgress = true` directly (l.68) with a comment claiming it avoids a race, then calls SetSaveState("", 0.0) (l.69). SetSaveState (presenter_methods.go:30-46) wraps its body in UpdateUI (async fyne.Do) and, in the progress>=0 branch — which 0.0 takes — also sets SaveInProgress=true (l.45). So the flag is written twice. The direct synchronous set IS load-bearing: checkSaveOperationState (l.104-110) reads SaveInProgress synchronously, and so does the dialog guard at l.202; the SetSaveState write is queued and would land later. The comment is accurate that a direct set is needed; the redundancy is that SetSaveState re-sets the same flag. The finding's framing ('comment overstates race protection') is slightly over-stated — the direct set genuinely matters for the synchronous read — but the core observation (two writes, one redundant) is real. Low value cleanup.

judgment.verifyReason: Both writes confirmed (direct l.68 + SetSaveState l.45); direct set is load-bearing for the synchronous check, so this is a taste/consolidation call not a clear bug.

judgment.title: completeSaveOperation uses blocking sleeps for a progress bar that is immediately hidden

It runs on the save goroutine (executeSaveOperation, started `go` at l.71), so the sleeps block that goroutine, not the UI thread. After it returns, the deferred finalizeSaveOperation (l.129 defer → l.250-258) calls SetSaveState("Save", -1) which hides the progress bar (progress<0 branch). So the 1.0 value plus 300ms is cosmetic, and the final 1.0 may be hidden before it visibly renders. judgment.verifyReason: Sleeps + 1.0 confirmed on the save goroutine, then finalize hides the bar; cosmetic timing the owner may want to keep for perceived feedback.

judgment.title: Redundant regexp.Compile inside SimplePatternToRegex
judgment.detail: Partly confirmed but low value to change. SimplePatternToRegex escapes every metacharacter in `\.+?()[]{}^$|` and only emits literal text or the safe group `(.*)`. For typical input the result is always valid, so regexp.Compile (95-97) essentially never fails. Note the escape set OMITS '*' (handled as wildcard) and does NOT escape '-' (harmless outside a class). The compile is a cheap belt-and-suspenders guard run only on infrequent user actions; the existing test (rule_dialog_test.go) even asserts the output always compiles. Removing it gives negligible benefit and slightly weakens defense against a future edit to the escape set. Best left as a documented guard.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Leave as-is (cheap safety net) or add a one-line comment that it is a defensive validity check; not worth a code change.
judgment.verifyReason: Compile is effectively-always-success for current construction, but it is a cheap intentional guard the test suite leans on; removing it is a judgment call with near-zero upside.

judgment.title: f.ReadFrom used for HTTP body copy without size guard
judgment.detail: Overstated. downloadGetFree (84-115) does copy the full body via f.ReadFrom(resp.Body) at line 106 with no byte cap. BUT the URL is hardcoded to the project's OWN repo: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Leadaxe/singbox-launcher/<branch>/bin/get_free.json (line 89, branch from constants.GetMyBranch()). It is not a user-supplied or arbitrary 'pinned-ref' endpoint, so the 'hostile endpoint streams arbitrary bytes' threat model does not hold — an attacker would need to control the maintainer's GitHub repo. The write goes to a .tmp then atomic-renames, and a 10s timeout bounds duration. Adding io.LimitReader is a cheap defensive nicety but not a real vulnerability; the security-flavored framing is unwarranted.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optional hardening only: f copy via io.LimitReader(resp.Body, ~1<<20) since the file is a small JSON list. Not security-urgent.

judgment.title: Redundant Del guard: IsRequired rows are already routed away from the Del button
judgment.detail: VERIFIED. The delBtn handler re-checks `rowsNow[rowIdx].IsPreset || rowsNow[rowIdx].IsRequired` and bails at line 528. The layout (593-599) routes IsPreset || IsRequired rows to the Edit+Reset arm (no delBtn rendered), and the regular arm (598) renders delBtn only for non-preset/non-required rows. So the IsPreset/IsRequired portion of the in-handler guard can never fire for a rendered delBtn; only the rowIdx>=len staleness check is meaningful. The finding itself frames this as belt-and-suspenders defensive redundancy, not a bug — which is accurate. Given index-staleness can shift rows between render and tap, keeping a defensive guard is reasonable, so this is a low-value taste call.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optionally simplify the delBtn guard to just the bounds check, or leave a one-line note that the IsPreset/IsRequired arm is belt-and-suspenders.
judgment.verifyReason: Confirmed delBtn only renders for non-preset/non-required rows (:598) so the :528 IsPreset/IsRequired arm is unreachable for it; but it is harmless defensive code against index staleness, so simplifying is an owner taste call.

judgment.title: Unused re-export type aliases in wizard_state_file.go
judgment.detail: PARTIALLY WRONG. Verified dead: SubscriptionMeta, UserInfo, ConnectionsSection, UpdateSpec, and the SourceType *type* (not the consts) — all 0 references project-wide. BUT the finding wrongly lists PersistedDNSState (line 40) as dead: it has live production callers — business/wizard_dns.go:21 (LoadPersistedWizardDNS param), business/dns_settings_vars.go:25 (MigrateDNSScalarsFromPersistedToSettingsVars param), and presentation/presenter_state.go:166. PersistedDNSState must be KEPT.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Drop SubscriptionMeta, UserInfo, ConnectionsSection, UpdateSpec, and the SourceType type alias. Do NOT remove PersistedDNSState (it is used in production). Keep Source, Defaults? (Defaults alias is 0-ref too — could also be dropped).
judgment.verifyReason: 5 of 6 aliases confirmed dead, but PersistedDNSState is live (3 prod callsites) — finding's dead-list is overstated. Defaults alias is also 0-ref (finding said keep it).

judgment.title: Two near-identical wrapper closures with intentional forward-reference dance
judgment.detail: Verified: createWizardTabs declares showAddRuleDialogWrapper (var) and createRulesTabWrapper (var) as mutually-recursive closures with explanatory comments to dodge a circular import; createRulesTabWrapper is assigned at 280-282, showAddRuleDialogWrapper at 285-287. CreateRulesTab is invoked twice — once inside createRulesTabWrapper (line 281) and again directly at line 292. Works but is a fragile var-dance. This is a refactor/taste call (medium effort), not a defect.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optionally pass the dialog-shower as a plain parameter to CreateRulesTab and construct the rules tab once, or extract the wiring into a named helper.
judgment.verifyReason: Structure confirmed (two recursive var closures + CreateRulesTab at 281 and 292), but it compiles and works; intentional circular-import workaround — owner taste call.

judgment.title: System-wide Live has filter/search/pause; per-process Live sub-tab has none
judgment.detail: Confirmed factually but this is a needs-judgment design call, not a defect. The system-wide liveView has kind chips, search, pause, clear, and the passes()/filteredIndices() pipeline (:137-309). The per-process liveList (per_process_view.go:63-97) renders v.liveItems raw with no filtering. formatEventRow is shared (window.go:250). The asymmetry is plausibly intentional: the per-process tab is already scoped to one target so a process/kind filter matters less, and it shows backfill/issue markers (:74-79) the system-wide one doesn't. Whether to unify is a taste/UX decision for the owner; medium effort is right and confidence medium is appropriate.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Decide intent: either document the asymmetry, or factor a shared event-list component (filter + reverse-index + row formatter) used by both tabs.
judgment.verifyReason: Asymmetry is real (live_view.go:137-309 has filters; per_process_view.go:63-97 renders raw) but plausibly intentional — per-process tab is already target-scoped and adds backfill/issue markers.

judgment.title: Over-verbose / duplicated comment blocks restating deadlock and UnselectAll mechanics
judgment.detail: Confirmed but verbose-by-intent; needs-judgment. The fyne.Do-from-UI-thread deadlock rationale appears 3 times in build() (window.go:105-110, :126-132, :172-180) and the 'Unselect immediately so re-clicking re-fires' note is near-verbatim in both OnSelected handlers (live_view.go:120-121 and per_process_view.go:83-84). The deadlock is a genuine Fyne 2.7 footgun and the repetition, while heavy, is arguably defensive given how easy it is to reintroduce. Whether to consolidate is owner taste — content is correct, just redundant. Severity low is right.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Consolidate the deadlock rationale into one note at the top of build() and reference it at the two later sites; reduce the duplicated UnselectAll comment to one shared one-liner.
judgment.verifyReason: Deadlock note genuinely repeats at window.go:105-110/:126-132/:172-180 and UnselectAll note at live_view.go:120 + per_process_view.go:83 — correct content, redundancy is a defensible taste call.

judgment.title: perProcessView.refresh holds no lock while mutating shared slices read by other goroutines
judgment.detail: Confirmed as a misleading-locking smell, not a data race. refresh() reassigns v.liveItems/domainsData/ipsData/connsData/savedData (:239-267) without taking v.mu, while liveList.OnSelected (:86-92) and savedList open.OnTapped read/write v.liveItems etc. under no... actually OnSelected DOES take v.mu (:86-92). refresh runs from the 1s ticker goroutine and the subscribe goroutine, but BOTH go through fyne.Do (:293, :302), so they execute serialized on the single Fyne UI thread. OnSelected also runs on the UI thread. So there is no actual concurrent access — the access is UI-thread-confined. The smell is that v.mu is taken in exactly one place (OnSelected :86-92) implying protection the writer side doesn't honor; it's the worst-of-both. Refuting the implied race but confirming the inconsistency. Low severity correct.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Drop v.mu from per_process_view entirely and add a comment that all access is UI-thread-confined via fyne.Do; OR take it consistently in refresh(). Don't leave the half-lock.
judgment.verifyReason: refresh() mutates slices unlocked but both callers (ticker :293, subscriber :302) and OnSelected (:86) all marshal via fyne.Do onto the single UI thread — no real race, just a misleading lone mutex at :86-92.

judgment.title: Toolbar overflow-menu helpers (export/copy) export from active OR newest-completed implicitly
judgment.detail: Confirmed as a real UX ambiguity; needs-judgment. currentExport() (:146-163) returns the ActiveSession if any, else falls back to the newest completed session (comp[len-1], :154). The per-process tab can be displaying an OLDER saved session the user opened (per_process_view.go open.OnTapped sets v.target without affecting profiler state), so Copy/Export can silently export a different session than what's on screen. The confirmation only reports event count (toolbar.go:179), not which session. This is a behavior/UX call for the owner, not a clear defect — exporting the active/newest is a defensible default. Low severity, medium confidence appropriate.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Either tie export to the currently-displayed session/target, or surface the exported session identity (target + start time) in the confirm dialog (toolbar.go:179) so the user knows what was copied.
judgment.verifyReason: currentExport() at toolbar.go:147-154 prefers active else newest-completed, decoupled from the per-process tab's displayed session; confirm at :179 shows only event count — real ambiguity but a defensible default / UX call.

judgment.title: SendCtrlBreak is exported on all 3 platforms but called nowhere
judgment.detail: Code shape confirmed: SendCtrlBreak exists on all 3 platforms (Windows real impl via GenerateConsoleCtrlEvent, darwin/linux error stubs) and grep across all *.go (incl tests) shows zero callers. HOWEVER the auditor missed that this is a DOCUMENTED, deliberately-retained API: release notes 0-9-8.md:25 explicitly justify keeping CREATE_NO_WINDOW disabled to preserve SendCtrlBreak graceful shutdown, and 0-8-2.md:12,37 describe 'Graceful stop via console (AttachConsole + CTRL_BREAK) when the core has a console; fallback to taskkill' as a shipped Windows feature. So the call path is currently severed but the owner has consciously kept the API and the PrepareCommand trade-off for it. This is not an accidental dead leftover safe to silently delete — it is a deliberately-parked feature.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Do NOT silently delete. Owner decision: either re-wire SendCtrlBreak into core/process_service.go Stop (restoring the documented AttachConsole+CTRL_BREAK graceful path) before taskkill, or if abandoning the feature, remove SendCtrlBreak AND re-enable CREATE_NO_WINDOW AND update release notes 0-9-8/0-8-2. The two are coupled by an explicit documented trade-off.
judgment.verifyReason: Zero callers confirmed by grep, but release notes 0-9-8.md:25 and 0-8-2.md:12,37 document it as an intentionally-retained graceful-shutdown API coupled to the PrepareCommand CREATE_NO_WINDOW decision — not accidental dead code.

judgment.title: power_darwin.go and power_linux.go are near-identical state machines
judgment.detail: Confirmed. Both files have byte-identical var blocks (powerCallbacksMu/sleepCallbacks/resumeCallbacks/listenerStarted/sleepingFlag/powerCtx/powerCtxCancel) and byte-identical bodies for IsSleeping, PowerContext, RegisterSleepCallback, RegisterPowerResumeCallback, StopPowerResumeListener. The sleep-edge (cancel+nil ctx, snapshot+fire sleepCallbacks) and resume-edge (new ctx, snapshot+fire resumeCallbacks) logic is duplicated; only the event source differs (IOKit cgo vs godbus). A fix to one (e.g. the dead-callback cleanup above) must be applied twice. This is a real maintainability cost but extraction is a non-trivial refactor with build-tag/cgo considerations — a taste call for the owner, not a defect.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Optionally extract a build-tag-free powerHub struct (mutex/slices/context + onSleepEdge/onResumeEdge/snapshot) in a shared file; darwin/linux/windows drive it from their event source. Reasonable but discretionary.
judgment.verifyReason: Side-by-side read confirms the duplicated state + identical Register/IsSleeping/PowerContext/Stop bodies and parallel edge handlers; real but the extraction is a discretionary refactor across build tags, not a clear-cut fix.

judgment.title: Message-loop APIs checked against ERROR_SUCCESS that they never set on success
judgment.detail: Code shape confirmed: lines 182-187 guard `err != nil && err != windows.ERROR_SUCCESS` on TranslateMessage and DispatchMessageW returns. syscall LazyProc.Call always returns a non-nil Errno, so the guard relies on it equalling ERROR_SUCCESS (0) on success. TranslateMessage/DispatchMessageW return BOOL/LRESULT and don't reliably set LastError on success, so a stale thread LastError could in principle surface a spurious Warn. In practice the immediately-preceding GetMessageW.Call (line 174) overwrites LastError, and on success it's typically 0, so spurious warns are unlikely but possible. Low-severity log-hygiene smell; the auditor's medium-confidence/low-severity framing is fair. Note the same pattern is used intentionally elsewhere (StopPowerResumeListener:238) and is harmless there.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Drop the err checks on TranslateMessage/DispatchMessageW (idiomatic Win32 ignores them), or gate on the documented return value rather than LastError.
judgment.verifyReason: The ERROR_SUCCESS guard on TranslateMessage/DispatchMessageW is real, but a spurious-warn is only a theoretical log-hygiene issue (preceding GetMessageW resets LastError) — a minor taste call, not a behavior bug.

judgment.title: wmic 'still present in Win10+ default install' comment is outdated
judgment.detail: Comment confirmed verbatim at lines 8-9: 'wmic is deprecated in modern Windows but still present in Win10+ default install — sufficient for our user base.' The factual claim about wmic being a removable Feature-on-Demand that Microsoft began dropping from default installs in Win11 24H2 is accurate to my knowledge; on such machines DeviceModel/DeviceOSVersion silently fall back to 'unknown'. This degradation is already handled gracefully (loadDeviceInfo substitutes 'unknown'), so it's a comment-accuracy issue plus an optional robustness upgrade, not a bug. Low severity — the values feed HWID-binding request headers (SPEC 061-F), so 'unknown' merely reduces fingerprint fidelity, it doesn't break functionality.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Update the comment to note wmic may be absent on recent Win11 (24H2+). Optionally migrate to PowerShell Get-CimInstance (Win32_OperatingSystem/Win32_ComputerSystem) or registry CurrentBuild reads, which are always present.
judgment.verifyReason: Comment text confirmed at lines 8-9 and the wmic-removal trend is real, but the impact is graceful 'unknown' fallback (already coded), so it's a doc-accuracy/robustness call for the owner rather than a defect.

judgment.title: Two no-op cgo accessor calls in the darwin power default branch
judgment.detail: PARTLY WRONG premise. Lines 233-234 (`_ = C.kMsgCanSystemSleep()` / `_ = C.kMsgSystemWillNotSleep()`) are confirmed as discard-only calls in the default branch. But the auditor's stated reason — that these accessors are 'used in the switch case expressions already (lines 198,216)' — is FALSE: lines 198/216 use kMsgSystemWillSleep and kMsgSystemHasPoweredOn, NOT kMsgCanSystemSleep/kMsgSystemWillNotSleep. Lines 233-234 are the ONLY Go references to those two C static functions. Deleting the lines would leave two unreferenced static C functions in the cgo preamble, which typically produces -Wunused-function warnings (cgo doesn't use -Werror by default, so likely not a build break, but not the trivial no-op the auditor describes). The cleanup is plausible but must also delete the two unused C accessor definitions (lines 87,89) to be clean — verify the build after.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: If removing lines 233-234, also delete the now-unreferenced C accessors kMsgCanSystemSleep (line 87) and kMsgSystemWillNotSleep (line 89) to avoid -Wunused-function noise; rebuild with cgo to confirm.
judgment.verifyReason: Lines 233-234 are discard-only, but the auditor's justification is factually wrong (the switch cases use different accessors) — these are the sole refs to those C funcs, so naive deletion leaves unused C statics; the fix is coupled, not a trivial no-op removal.

judgment.title: eventFromConn / eventsFromLogLine acquire p.mu in many tiny separate critical sections per event
judgment.detail: Verified. eventFromConn locks/unlocks at 223-231 (dnsByIP inference) then again at 234-238 (dnsAccum). The DNS-resolve path in eventsFromLogLine locks separately at 247-249 (process map), 256-258 (dnsAccum append), inside fillAttribution 315-322, 271-277 (dnsByIP write), and 280-284 (dnsAccum snapshot) — up to 5 acquisitions for one event. It is correct because runJoin is the single producer (no interleaving writer), so a backfill spanning these sub-sections cannot be torn by a concurrent producer; readers (Snapshot/Aggregate) only see consistent committed state per critical section. The churn is a clarity/micro-perf nit, not a bug.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Coalesce each event's reads/writes into one critical section for readability.
judgment.verifyReason: Lock pattern is as described and harmless given single-producer runJoin; whether to coalesce is a readability/taste call for the owner.

judgment.title: MatchedVia labeled 'router_log' for Clash-API-sourced attribution
judgment.detail: Verified. In eventFromConn (profiler.go:203-217) ProcessPath is taken from c.Metadata.ProcessPath (Clash /connections metadata, i.e. sing-box find_process), then at 218-220 tagged MatchedVia="router_log". fillAttribution (314-322) uses the same "router_log" label for the genuinely log-sourced connProcessMap. So two distinct attribution sources collapse to one debug label. MatchedVia is a diagnostic field only; correctness of attribution is unaffected.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Use a distinct MatchedVia value (e.g. 'clash_meta') in eventFromConn to distinguish Clash-metadata from router-log attribution.
judgment.verifyReason: Label conflation is real, but MatchedVia is a debug-only aid with no functional impact; relabeling is an owner preference.

judgment.title: Rolling-buffer GC assumes p.roll is TS-sorted, but events are appended in arrival order
judgment.detail: Verified the mechanism. dispatch() head-evicts while p.roll[0].TS.Before(now-60s) (332-335) and Snapshot() filters by !e.TS.Before(cutoff) over the same slice (498-504). Poller events stamp TS=d.At (poll wall-clock, profiler.go:205 via eventFromConn) while log events stamp TS=ll.TS (parsed log timestamp). If a log line's parsed TS lags the latest poll event, the ascending-TS invariant the head-eviction relies on is violated, so eviction can stop early (one stale head element survives a cycle) or Snapshot's linear filter can include/exclude a wrong edge element. Impact is small (near-real-time timestamps, bounded buffer) and the invariant is genuinely implicit/undocumented.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Stamp rolling-buffer entries with a single arrival clock for ordering, or scan the whole slice instead of relying on head ordering. At minimum document the invariant.
judgment.verifyReason: Two-clock TS sourcing is real and the head-eviction does assume sort order, but the practical window for a stale/edge element is tiny and self-correcting; owner's call whether to harden.

judgment.title: Front-slice eviction (s = s[1:]) retains backing array head, leaking capacity under churn
judgment.detail: Verified all four sites: profiler.go:334 and 337 (p.roll = p.roll[1:]), session.go:57 and 62 (s.events = s.events[1:]). Reslicing from index 1 keeps the backing array head referenced until append reallocates, and the stale-by-age loop in Append (session.go:56-59) reslices one element at a time. Capacity is hard-bounded by rollingBufferMax (3000) and maxEventsPerSession (50000), so this is a steady-state memory/copy inefficiency, not a leak in the unbounded sense.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Use a ring buffer or copy-compact survivors to the front when trimming many head elements.
judgment.verifyReason: Reslice pattern and capacity-retention are real Go behavior, but bounded by the caps; this is a micro-optimization the owner may reasonably decline.

judgment.title: NewCustom replaces canvas-global OnTypedKey, fragile under stacked dialogs
judgment.detail: Partially confirmed but largely a non-issue in practice. The code does swap the canvas-global OnTypedKey (dialogs.go:57-58) and captures originalOnTypedKey at construction, restoring both in the ESC branch (62-66) and in SetOnClosed (76-82). Two concerns: (1) the double-restore on ESC is real but harmless/idempotent — both branches set the same handler; (2) the stacked-dialog clobbering is real in principle (single canvas handler, stale 'original' if two dismiss-ESC dialogs coexist). However NewCustom dialogs are modal; ~14 callsites but no evidence two dismiss-text dialogs stack on the same canvas simultaneously. This is a latent fragility / taste call, not a demonstrated bug. Severity overstated at medium.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Drop the inline ESC-branch restore (let SetOnClosed be the single restore point), or use a per-dialog key listener instead of mutating the canvas-global handler. Optional.
judgment.verifyReason: Code matches description (global swap + double restore). Stacking is an unproven edge case for modal dialogs; double-restore is idempotent. Lowered medium->low.

judgment.title: Triple-layered request timeout with redundant http.Client.Timeout
judgment.detail: Confirmed factually: clashHTTPClient sets Client.Timeout = 20s (line 156) and every request also wraps the context with WithTimeout(ctx, 20s) (lines 263,352,495,548). GetDelay additionally passes timeout=5000 to Clash (line 547). The two 20s deadlines are redundant double-bookkeeping. However framing as 'premature-opt' is a stretch — it's a minor redundancy/clarity issue, not a performance optimization. The audit itself notes it's 'not functionally broken'; the context deadline is what's needed for PowerContext sleep cancellation, the Client.Timeout is a harmless coarse net. This is a taste call for the owner (dropping Client.Timeout removes a safety net for any future code path that bypasses the per-request WithTimeout).
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Either drop Client.Timeout to rely on the context deadline, or keep it as a coarse net set above the context deadline; document intent either way.
judgment.verifyReason: Confirmed both 20s deadlines exist (line 156 + lines 263/352/495/548), but it's a harmless redundancy/clarity nit, not a real perf problem — owner's call whether the Client.Timeout safety net is worth keeping.

judgment.title: ProxyInfo.DisplayOrName re-normalizes Name on a fallback path that is never reached in production
judgment.detail: Partially confirmed but overstated. DisplayOrName (316-325): if DisplayName!="" return it, else NormalizeProxyDisplay(Name) with Name fallback. GetProxiesInGroup (444-448) always sets DisplayName (disp defaults to name when norm yields empty), so for proxies produced there DisplayName is always non-empty and the else-branch is dead. BUT DisplayOrName is an exported method on an exported struct — it is a public-API guard by design, and the audit's own text concedes this ('it is exported and cheap', 'public-API guard'). Calling it 'unnecessary' is wrong: it's intentional defensive code for the exported contract. Not worth removing; if anything keep as-is or add a doc note. Low value finding.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Leave as-is (defensive guard for the exported method) or add a one-line doc noting the producer guarantees DisplayName; do not delete the fallback from an exported API.
judgment.verifyReason: Confirmed the else-branch is unreachable for GetProxiesInGroup output (444-448 always sets DisplayName), but DisplayOrName is an exported method so the fallback is a deliberate public-API guard, not dead code to strip.

judgment.title: rebuild-config handler doc-comment promises {"rebuilt":bool} but returns only {"ok":true}
judgment.detail: The handleRebuildConfig doc-comment (server.go:286-287) documents the response as `{"ok":true,"rebuilt":false}` / `{"ok":true,"rebuilt":true}`, but the handler at line 302 returns only `{"ok":true}`. API.md:151 already correctly footnotes this discrepancy ('doc-comment ... обещает {rebuilt:bool} ... но handler возвращает только {ok:true}'), so the external doc is accurate — the mismatch is internal to the code, where the in-code comment is the misleading artifact. This is a behavior/intent call (the comment may reflect a planned-but-unimplemented field), hence needs-judgment.
judgment.suggestedCleanup: Either return rebuilt bool from RebuildConfigIfDirty and include it in the JSON, or fix the server.go doc-comment to match the actual {"ok":true} response (and then drop the API.md caveat).
judgment.verifyReason: server.go:286-287 doc-comment promises {"ok":true,"rebuilt":false/true}; handler at server.go:302 writes only map{"ok":true}; API.md:151 already footnotes the mismatch — fix is a judgment call (return the field vs amend the comment).

refuted.title: Stale `_ = subscription.IsSubscriptionURL` keep-alive in source row loop
refuted.detail: Refuted as written. grep shows subscription.IsSubscriptionURL at line 174 is the ONLY reference to the subscription package in source_tab.go (import at line 34). The finding's premise ('the package is imported and used elsewhere in the file') is false, and the suggested fix ('remove the line') would make the import unused and break compilation. The placement inside the per-row IIFE is a mild smell (better as a package-level `var _ = ...`) and the comment mentioning classifyInputLines is inaccurate, but the statement itself is load-bearing, not removable noise.
refuted.suggestedCleanup: Do not remove. If cleanup is wanted, replace with a package-level `var _ = subscription.IsSubscriptionURL` (or actually use the import) and fix the misleading comment.
refuted.verifyReason: grep proves line 174 is the sole subscription.* reference in the file; removing it (the suggested fix) breaks the build, so the 'redundant, import used elsewhere' claim is wrong.

refuted.title: Duplicated ad-hoc interface{ SetToolTip(string) } type-assertion idiom
refuted.detail: PARTIALLY REFUTED on premise, but uncovers a real latent bug. The finding claims the assertion is "defensive code for a type that is known to satisfy the interface" because HoverForwardButton supposedly has SetToolTip. That is FALSE: HoverForwardButton embeds widget.Button by value (internal/fynewidget/hover_forward.go:27-28), NOT ttwidget.Button — only HoverForwardTTButton (line 99-100) embeds ttwidget.Button. I confirmed at runtime with a throwaway test: `interface{}(NewHoverForwardButton(...)).(interface{ SetToolTip(string) })` returns ok=FALSE. So the duplicated assertion does NOT set tooltips — it silently no-ops for the reorder buttons (402-406, upBtn/downBtn) and the resetBtn (576-578). The package doc itself says "For ttwidget tooltips on plain actions, prefer HoverForwardTTButton". The dedup suggestion ("call SetToolTip directly on the concrete type") would not even compile. The real issue is a dead/broken tooltip, not redundant defensiveness.
refuted.suggestedCleanup: Do NOT collapse to a direct concrete call (won't compile). Either switch these buttons to HoverForwardTTButton (which has SetToolTip) so the reorder/reset tooltips actually appear, or remove the dead assertion blocks entirely if the tooltips are not wanted.
refuted.verifyReason: Finding's core premise is wrong: HoverForwardButton embeds widget.Button (hover_forward.go:27), and a runtime assert proved it does NOT satisfy SetToolTip — the suggested 'direct call on concrete type' is impossible; these tooltips are actually dead no-ops.

refuted.title: KillProcess tries taskkill without /F first, then retries with /F — likely always double-runs
refuted.detail: Code shape confirmed: KillProcess (42-52) and KillProcessByPID (54-65) both run taskkill without /F, and on error retry with /F plus a DebugLog. BUT the auditor's framing as wasteful accident is contradicted by the documented design: release notes 0-8-2.md:12,37 explicitly describe 'taskkill tries without /F first, then with /F on error' as the intended Windows stop behavior (paired with the AttachConsole+CTRL_BREAK graceful path). The claim that the non-/F attempt 'rarely succeeds and is mostly wasted work' is an unverifiable runtime hypothesis — the auditor cannot observe Windows runtime here, and a graceful (non-/F) close is the deliberate first choice to let the process flush. This is intentional resilience design, not a smell.
refuted.suggestedCleanup: No change recommended. The two-phase taskkill is a documented intentional graceful-then-force pattern (release notes 0-8-2). If the owner ever measures that graceful close never succeeds for -H windowsgui sing-box, only then consider collapsing to /F.
refuted.verifyReason: Release notes 0-8-2.md:12,37 document the without-/F-then-/F sequence as deliberate graceful-then-force design; the 'always double-runs / wasted work' claim is an unverifiable runtime hypothesis, not a defect.

refuted.title: Redundant 4096-byte cap after a zero-size check that already bounds the buffer
refuted.detail: Code shape confirmed: getRegistryPropertyW returns on requiredSize==0 (209-211) then caps requiredSize>4096 (213-215). The auditor self-rates this confidence:low and effort:trivial and explicitly calls it 'acceptable as defense-in-depth.' This is defensive bounds-checking against a SetupAPI-returned size before allocating make([]uint16, requiredSize/2+1) — entirely reasonable hardening, not a defect. The auditor's own framing concedes it's fine. Not worth a finding.
refuted.suggestedCleanup: Leave as-is. If desired purely for consistency, the 4096 magic number could be a named const like maxInstanceIDChars, but this is cosmetic.
refuted.verifyReason: The 4096 cap is sensible defense-in-depth before an unsafe allocation sized from an OS-returned value; the auditor itself concedes it's acceptable — not an unnecessary check worth changing.

refuted.title: dialogs button handlers call fyne.Do from inside the UI thread
refuted.detail: Refuted as stated. The factual mechanism is wrong for Fyne v2.7.4 (the pinned version per go.mod). The thread-affinity warning lives in async.EnsureNotMain (goroutine.go:33) which is ONLY reached on the wait=true path (DoFromGoroutine wait=true -> DoAndWait). Plain fyne.Do is non-wait: thread.go:18 -> DoFromGoroutine(fn,false) -> glfw driver.go:73 runOnMainWithWait(f,false), which never calls EnsureNotMain and just enqueues funcData to funcQueue (loop.go:55). The dialogs package uses fyne.Do (non-wait) everywhere, not DoAndWait, so it never logs the warning, even from the main goroutine, and never deadlocks (it defers to the next loop iteration). The 'nested fyne.Do is unnecessary indirection' part is a minor taste point, but the claimed harm (warnings/affinity error) does not occur. Self-rated confidence low.
refuted.suggestedCleanup: No action required for correctness. Optionally simplify by calling dialog.ShowError directly in handlers already on the UI thread, but this is cosmetic.
refuted.verifyReason: Fyne v2.7.4 source: warning is in EnsureNotMain, reachable only via wait=true; non-wait fyne.Do (used here) just enqueues, never warns from main.
